    Home › Aircraft › Airbus A340

    Airbus A340

    Airbus · Widebody · Out of production (2011) — in service

    Airbus's four-engine long-hauler.

    Length75.36 m
    Wingspan63.45 m
    Range14,630 km
    MTOW380 t
    Typical seats380
    Built377
    Our numbers

    Derived metrics

    Computed by aircraft.fyi from the core specification — see how we calculate these.

    Seats per metre#6 of 30
    5.04 seats/m typical seats ÷ fuselage length
    Range per tonne#26 of 40
    38.5 km/t maximum range ÷ MTOW in tonnes
    Span-to-length ratio#32 of 43
    0.84 wingspan ÷ fuselage length
    Weight per metre#8 of 43
    5.04 t/m MTOW ÷ fuselage length
    Range in hours#3 of 40
    16.0 h maximum range ÷ cruise speed
    Weight per seat#6 of 30
    1000 kg MTOW ÷ typical seats
    Sense of scale

    How big is it, really?

    The Airbus A340's wingspan, against things you already know the size of.

    Airbus A34063.45 m
    Football pitch (width)68 m
    Blue whale25 m
    New Routemaster bus11.2 m
    You (probably)1.75 m
    #4 of 43 by length → #6 by weight → #19 by speed →
    Specification

    The numbers

    Length (-600)75.36 m
    Wingspan63.45 m
    Height17.93 m
    MTOW380,000 kg
    Range14,630 km
    Typical seats380 (3-class)
    Engines4 × Trent 556
    First flight (A340)25 October 1991
    First flight (-600)23 April 2001
    Built (all variants)377

    Compare it

    vs Airbus A350 vs Boeing 747 Compare with anything →
    ← PreviousAirbus A330 Next →Airbus A350

    Last verified: 2026-07-14 · Spot an error? business@luck.fyi

    The story

    What makes it different

    The A340 was designed for a world where twins weren't trusted over oceans. Four engines meant freedom from ETOPS restrictions — it could take the shortest route across any water, any desert, anywhere — and in the 1990s that made it the queen of the very long haul. Its marketing line was blunt: four engines for long haul.

    The A340-600 of 2002 stretched the family to 75.36 metres, snatching the "world's longest airliner" title from the 747 and holding it until the 747-8 flew in 2010. But the same engines that gave it freedom became its downfall: as ETOPS rules relaxed and big twins like the 777 matched its range on half the engines, the A340's fuel economics collapsed. Production ended in 2011 after 377 aircraft — the A350 is its successor.

    It flies on in smaller numbers than its Boeing rivals, led by Lufthansa's reactivated fleet, with a long tail of charter, government and VIP operators who still value what it was built for: range without restrictions.

    Variants

    The Airbus A340 line

    A340-200 / -300
    The originals, CFM56-powered — the -300 was the family's best-seller.
    A340-500
    The ultra-long-ranger — flew the world's longest route, Singapore–Newark, before the A350ULR existed.
    A340-600
    The 75.36 m stretch — world's longest airliner from 2002 to 2010. Spec shown above.
    Operators

    Who flies the Airbus A340

    The top 6 operators by fleet size, of ≈20 worldwide — the rest are aggregated below. Counts marked ≈ are approximate, verified 2026-07-14.

    LHLufthansaThe largest remaining operator — A340-300s reactivated after the pandemic to cover long-haul demand.≈17→
    IRMahan AirOne of the largest remaining A340 fleets.≈10
    CHSWISSA340-300s on their final tour before the A350 replaces them.≈4
    CHEdelweiss AirLeisure long-haul from Zurich.≈4
    MTMaleth-AeroCharter and wet-lease A340s.≈3
    VEConviasaVenezuela's flag carrier.≈2
    Other operatorsA further ≈15 operators — largely charter, government and VIP fleets, including several head-of-state aircraft. Passenger numbers shrink each year as the A350 replaces the type.≈25

    About the data. Every specification is compiled from manufacturer documents and cited per row. Operator fleet counts are approximate snapshots, marked ≈ and dated; individually verified figures carry a ✓. All silhouettes are original works of aircraft.fyi, drawn to true scale. Corrections and enquiries: business@luck.fyi — we fix errors visibly, not silently.
